title: Apium graveolens (Celery)
medicine: Celery (Apium graveolens) has many health benefits, such as reducing inflammation, improving digestion, lowering blood pressure, regulating blood sugar levels and aiding weight loss. It is also believed to have anti-cancer properties and can improve cognitive function. Additionally, celery may help with respiratory issues such as asthma and bronchitis.

The medicinal constituents of celery include flavonoids, antioxidants, and minerals such as potassium, magnesium, and phosphorus. The flavonoids in celery have been shown to have anti-inflammatory properties, while the antioxidants help protect against cell damage. The minerals in celery help regulate blood pressure and support the heart's health.

The most commonly used parts of celery for medicinal purposes are the leaves, stems, and roots. Celery juice is also commonly consumed for its health benefits. Celery can be taken as a dietary supplement in the form of capsules or powders. It can also be added to smoothies or eaten raw.

Possible side effects of using celery include allergic reactions and digestive issues such as stomach irritation and bloating. Some people may experience headaches or dizziness when consuming large amounts of celery. Additionally, celery can interact with certain medications, so it is important to consult a healthcare professional before taking celery supplements.

To ensure safe use of celery, it is recommended to consume it in moderation and to consult a healthcare professional before using it as a remedy for any health conditions. It is also important to properly store and prepare celery to prevent contamination with harmful bacteria. Additionally, individuals with allergies to celery should avoid consuming it or taking supplements containing it.
horticulture: Celery (Apium graveolens) is a semi-hardy perennial that requires full sun and well-draining loam soil with a pH of 6.0 to 7.0. The plant grows best in cooler temperatures, preferring temperatures between 15°C and 25°C. Celery requires ample moisture throughout the growing season, with an annual rainfall of 30-50 cm.

The seeds of celery can be sown directly into the soil in early spring or late summer. The seeds should be planted about 1 cm deep and spaced about 15 cm apart. It is important to cover the seeds with a thin layer of soil and gently press it down to remove any air pockets. The seeds will germinate in about one week, and the seedlings should emerge in two weeks.

Once established, celery requires regular watering to maintain moisture levels. The plant should be fertilized every 4-6 weeks with a nitrogen-rich fertilizer, following the package instructions for application rates. Celery is sensitive to frost and should be protected in colder temperatures. To do this, the plant can be covered with a layer of row cover or mulch.

Celery can be harvested after about 60 days of growth when it reaches a height of at least 30 cm. The stems should be cut back to a base of 15-20 cm to encourage new growth. Celery can also be harvested throughout the growing season by cutting off individual stalks as needed.

Celery is susceptible to several pests, including aphids, whiteflies, and caterpillars. These pests can cause damage to the foliage and should be monitored regularly. Celery can also develop several diseases, including bacterial blight, fungal leaf spot, and stem rot. To prevent these problems, it is important to practice good garden hygiene, such as removing dead leaves and debris from the area around the plant and avoiding overhead irrigation.
botany: Celery, with botanical name Apium graveolens, belongs to the domain Eukarya, kingdom Plantae, phylum Tracheophyta, class Magnoliopsida, order Apiales, family Apiaceae, genus Apium, and species Apium graveolens. Common names for celery include Chinese celery (Apium chinense), Japanese celery (Apium tateyanense), Indian celery (Apium graveolens var. pachyrrhizum). It's a perennial herb that grows to a height of 0.5-1 meter and has long, thin stalks that are covered in tiny hairs.

Celery is characterized by its distinctive stalks, which are elongated and have a smooth, ribbed appearance. The leaves are dark green, opposite, and simple with lobed margins. Celery also produces small, white flowers that bloom on the plant's stems. The fruit of celery is an achenium, which contains several seeds.

There are several variants of celery, each with different characteristics. Chinese celery (Apium chinense) has a more tender texture and a milder flavor compared to traditional celery (Apium graveolens). Japanese celery (Apium tateyanense) has a slightly sweet taste and a thin stalk that is more delicate than traditional celery. Indian celery (Apium graveolens var. pachyrrhizum) has a thicker, fleshy stem and a milder flavor compared to traditional celery.

Celery is native to the eastern coast of Asia, where it has been cultivated for thousands of years. It is now grown in many parts of the world, including Europe, North America, and Australia. Celery grows best in well-drained soil with plenty of sunlight and moisture. It is a popular crop because of its high nutritional value and versatility in cooking.

The life cycle of celery begins with the seed, which germinates in the soil and grows into a small plant. As the plant grows, it produces leaves that absorb sunlight and water to feed the plant. After blooming, the plant produces fruit, which contains seeds that can be used to grow new plants. Celery is a perennial herb, meaning it can live for several years and produce crops year after year.
intro: Health advantages attributed to celery, an often overlooked vegetable, are numerous and impressive. Its natural alkaloids help calm an agitated digestive system while its high water content aids in hydration and flushing out toxins. Celery is a rich source of antioxidants, particularly phenolic flavonoids, which offer anti-inflammatory properties and combat oxidative stress. Further to these merits, celery contains nutrients essential for heart health: potassium, fiber, and phytochemicals like phthalides that promote blood vessel dilation and a subsequent decrease in hypertension risks. These crunchy stalks are also low in calories yet high in dietary fiber, making them an ideal inclusion in weight management plans.

The cultivation of celery requires attentive horticultural conditions to achieve the desired crisp texture and vibrant color. As a cool-season crop, celery thrives in temperate climates with consistent temperatures between 18-24°C during the day and a dip by approximately 5-6°C at night, ensuring optimal growth. To prevent bolting, or the undesirable flower stalk emergence, growers maintain consistently moist soil, protect young plants from harsh sunlight with row covers, and ensure an adequate spacing between individual plants for air circulation and sufficient humidity.

Botanically, celery belongs to the Apiaceae family, commonly known as the carrot or parsley family, owing to its affinity with these herbs in appearance and flavor profiles. Apium graveolens is a biennial herb manipulated over centuries through selective breeding to exhibit distinct stalk vegetable characteristics. The edible parts are the succulent, ribbed green stems or petioles, culminating from an inconspicuous, flat-leaved rosette resembling its wild relative, maritime celery (Apium graveolens var. secalinum). While it naturally self-seeds, cultivated celery is propagated vegetatively, usually by dividing rootstocks or through tissue culture methods to maintain uniformity and prevent the reversion to its wild form.
